explicit_text: Adly Iskandar was the man who caught my eye on the beach and eventually became my husband. Following Egyptian tradition, he approached my father to ask for my hand in marriage before even speaking to me. He later became a doctor, working in London and then Boston after we moved to the United States in 1969. Despite the challenges of redoing his medical residency in the US, he persevered and built a successful career.

explicit_text: Egypt is where my story began, and where the traditional customs shaped my early life, including how Adly approached my father for marriage. The religious tensions between Islam and Christianity there in the late 1960s made life difficult for us, especially after the 6-Day War in 1967, which devastated the economy. These challenges contributed to our decision to move to the United States in 1969. Despite leaving, Egypt remained a central part of my identity and family history.

These challenges contributed to our decision to move to the United States in 1969. Despite leaving, that country remained a central part of my identity and family history.

explicit_text: I lived in Paris for ten years while pursuing my PhD at an elite university, focusing my thesis on a French writer. This period was just before and after my marriage to Adly, who was also advancing his medical career. Paris was a formative place for both of us, and we often traveled back and forth between Paris and London before moving to the US. It was a vibrant time that shaped much of my academic and personal life.

explicit_text: London was where Adly worked as a doctor after our time in Paris. We moved back and forth between Paris and London for several years before deciding to relocate to the United States in 1969. London was an important step in Adly’s medical career, but ultimately we sought better opportunities and stability in the US due to the political and religious climate in Egypt.

This city was an important step in his medical career, but ultimately we sought better opportunities and stability in the US due to the political and religious climate in our home country.

explicit_text: We moved to the United States in 1969, seeking a safer and more stable environment away from Egypt’s religious conflicts and economic troubles after the 6-Day War. Adly took a job as a doctor in Boston, though he had to redo his medical residency to practice here. I worked as a trilingual translator and later as a docent at the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston before becoming a successful realtor. Our immigration process was relatively smooth due to our education and skills, and we eventually became citizens.

explicit_text: Boston became our home when Adly accepted a medical position there after we moved to the US in 1969. I found a meaningful role working as a trilingual translator for immigration courts and as a docent at the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston. Over time, I also became a successful realtor and helped connect Egyptian and French communities in the city. Boston is where I was laid to rest next to Adly, surrounded by the family we built.

explicit_text: The 6-Day War in 1967 was a pivotal event that deeply affected Egypt’s economy and social fabric. Its aftermath created a tense religious climate between Muslims and Christians, which influenced our decision to leave Egypt. Thousands of Egyptians, including us, immigrated to the United States in the years following the war, seeking better opportunities and safety. This event marked a turning point in our lives and the broader Egyptian diaspora.
implicit_text: The brief conflict in June 1967 was a pivotal event that deeply affected the economy and social fabric of my home country. Its aftermath created a tense religious climate between Muslims and Christians, which influenced our decision to leave. Thousands of people from that nation, including us, immigrated to the United States in the years following the war, seeking better opportunities and safety. This event marked a turning point in our lives and the broader diaspora.

explicit_text: I worked as a docent at the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston after we settled in the city. This role allowed me to engage with the community and share my knowledge and passion for art. It was one of the meaningful ways I contributed to my new home while also balancing my work as a translator and later as a realtor. The museum was a special place where I felt connected and valued.

explicit_text: Palm Springs, California, became important to our family when my brother started a date ranch there. This venture helped keep our family connected even as many of us moved to the United States. While Adly and I lived primarily in Boston, Palm Springs was a place where part of our extended family gathered and maintained their roots. It was a symbol of family unity across different locations.
